content.text: is the integration of the Model Context Protocol into Claude Code's CLI, giving Claude Code access to
hundreds of external tools, databases, and APIs
through MCP servers. This transforms Claude Code from a pure coding assistant into a
that can interact directly with external systems using natural language.
What MCP Enables in Claude Code
With connected MCP servers, you can ask Claude Code to:
"Add the feature described in JIRA issue ENG-4521 and create a PR on GitHub"
"Check Sentry and Statsig to check the usage of the feature described in ENG-4521"
"Find emails of 10 random users who used feature ENG-4521, based on our PostgreSQL database"
"Update our standard email template based on the new Figma designs that were posted in Slack"
"Create Gmail drafts inviting these 10 users to a feedback session about the new feature"
MCP servers can push messages into your session (Telegram, Discord, webhook events)
— use HTTP servers instead where available.

# Basic syntax
claude mcp add --transport http <name> <url>

# Real example: Connect to Notion
claude mcp add --transport http notion https://mcp.notion.com/mcp

# With Bearer token
claude mcp add --transport http secure-api https://api.example.com/mcp \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer your-token"

# Basic syntax
claude mcp add [options] <name> -- <command> [args...]

# Real example: Add Airtable server
claude mcp add --env AIRTABLE_API_KEY=YOUR_KEY --transport stdio airtable \
  -- npx -y airtable-mcp-server
— the double dash separates Claude's options from the server command.
Requires macOS or WSL. Interactive dialog to select servers.

# List all configured servers
claude mcp list

# Get details for a specific server
claude mcp get github

# Remove a server
claude mcp remove github

# (within Claude Code) Check server status
/mcp

panel shows:
- Tool count next to each connected server
- Flags servers advertising tools capability but exposing no tools

(when same server is defined multiple times):
1. Local scope (highest precedence)
2. Project scope
3. User scope
4. Plugin-provided servers
5. claude.ai connectors
Many cloud MCP servers require authentication. Claude Code supports OAuth 2.0:

# Add server requiring auth
claude mcp add --transport http sentry https://mcp.sentry.dev/mcp

- Tokens stored securely in system keychain (macOS) or credentials file
- Use "Clear authentication" in

to revoke access
- OAuth works with HTTP servers only

For HTTP/SSE servers that disconnect mid-session:
-

with exponential backoff (1s → 2s → 4s → 8s → 16s)
- Server appears as

while reconnecting
- After 5 failures: marked as
Initial connection also retries up to
on transient errors (5xx, connection refused, timeout).

Enabled by default, MCP tool search allows Claude to:
- Load tools

from large MCP servers
- Wait for connecting servers inside the
(Note: Earlier docs mentioned 100K token limit for file writing in sandbox).
Using Claude Code as an MCP Server
You can run Claude Code itself as an MCP server for other applications:

# Start as stdio MCP server
claude mcp serve
Plugins can bundle MCP servers automatically:

- Bundled distribution (tools + servers together)
- Automatic setup (no manual configuration)
- Team consistency (everyone gets same tools)

If logged into Claude Code with Claude.ai account:
- MCP servers added at

are automatically available
- Team/Enterprise: only admins can add servers
= CLI integration for external tool access via natural language
for HTTP/SSE (5 attempts); stdio doesn't auto-reconnect
use Claude Code as MCP server
Understanding the internals of mcp in claude code helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Production experience with mcp in claude code has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Deploying mcp in claude code in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.

MCP in Claude Code is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.
